subject: AP Psychology
theme: The nervous system coordinates all behavior and mental processes through electrochemical signals passed between billions of specialized cells organized into complex structures.
core_concepts: Neurons communicate via electrochemical signals: dendrites receive input, the axon transmits the action potential, and axon terminals release neurotransmitters across the synapse.
The action potential follows the all-or-none principle — once threshold (~$-55$ mV) is reached from resting potential (~$-70$ mV), the neuron fires at full strength regardless of stimulus intensity.
Neurotransmitters (dopamine, serotonin, acetylcholine, GABA, glutamate, norepinephrine, endorphins) have specific functions; imbalances are linked to psychological disorders.
The central nervous system (brain + spinal cord) processes information; the peripheral nervous system (somatic + autonomic) connects it to the body and organs.
The autonomic nervous system divides into the sympathetic (fight-or-flight arousal) and parasympathetic (rest-and-digest calming) branches that work in opposition.
The brain's cerebral cortex contains four lobes — frontal (executive function, motor), parietal (somatosensory), temporal (auditory, memory), and occipital (vision) — each with specialized functions.
Brain plasticity allows neural reorganization after injury or learning; critical periods exist for language and sensory development, but plasticity continues throughout life.
Research methods in biopsychology include lesion studies, EEG, CT, MRI, fMRI, and PET scans — each with distinct advantages for studying brain structure or function.

key_terms.definition: A nerve cell that receives and transmits electrical and chemical signals; the basic building block of the nervous system.
Branching extensions of a neuron that receive incoming signals from other neurons and conduct them toward the cell body.
The long fiber extending from the cell body that carries action potentials away from the soma toward the axon terminals.
A fatty insulating layer around axons that speeds up neural transmission via saltatory conduction; produced by glial cells.
An electrical impulse that travels down the axon when the neuron's membrane potential crosses threshold (~$-55$ mV).
The tiny gap between the axon terminal of one neuron and the dendrite of another where neurotransmitters are released.
A chemical messenger released into the synapse that excites or inhibits the postsynaptic neuron (e.g., dopamine, serotonin, GABA).
The process by which neurotransmitters are reabsorbed by the presynaptic neuron, ending the signal; targeted by SSRIs and SNRIs.
Neurotransmitter involved in reward, motivation, and motor control; linked to Parkinson's (deficit) and schizophrenia (excess).
Neurotransmitter that regulates mood, sleep, and appetite; low levels are associated with depression.
Neurotransmitter involved in muscle activation, attention, and memory; deficit linked to Alzheimer's disease.
The main inhibitory neurotransmitter in the brain; reduces neural activity. Low GABA is linked to anxiety disorders.
Natural opioid neurotransmitters that reduce pain perception and produce feelings of pleasure; released during exercise.
Division of the autonomic nervous system that arouses the body for fight-or-flight responses (increased heart rate, dilated pupils).
Division of the autonomic nervous system that calms the body after stress, promoting rest-and-digest functions.
The wrinkled outer layer of the cerebrum responsible for higher-order thinking, voluntary movement, language, and sensory processing.
Brain lobe behind the forehead; controls executive functions, planning, decision-making, personality, and voluntary movement (motor cortex).
Region in the left frontal lobe that controls speech production; damage causes Broca's aphasia (understands but cannot produce fluent speech).
Region in the left temporal lobe that controls language comprehension; damage causes fluent but meaningless speech.
Limbic system structure involved in processing emotions, especially fear and aggression.
Limbic system structure essential for forming new explicit (declarative) memories; damage causes anterograde amnesia.
Brain structure that regulates homeostasis — body temperature, hunger, thirst, sleep, and the endocrine system via the pituitary gland.
The thick band of nerve fibers connecting the left and right cerebral hemispheres, enabling communication between them.
The brain's ability to reorganize and form new neural connections in response to learning, experience, or injury.
Studies by Sperry and Gazzaniga on patients with severed corpus callosum, revealing lateralization of brain functions.

exam_tip: FRQ and MCQ questions frequently present a case study describing a patient with specific symptoms — you must identify the brain region or neurotransmitter involved and explain its normal function to earn full credit.
